requirement_sections.heading: Mathematics Program
content: The Department of Mathematics serves all students at the university. Mathematics majors receive a comprehensive foundation in abstract and applied mathematics as preparation for graduate school or a professional career. Minors in Mathematics receive the mathematical foundation needed for advanced work in their major field.
The department also provides non-majors with courses for their major or with courses for their General Education program.
Develop effective thinking and communication skills
Learn to link applications and theory
Develop mathematical independence and experience open-ended inquiry
items: Present information in a clear, precise and organized manner both verbally and in writing.
Use and compare analytical, visual, and numerical perspectives in exploring mathematics.
Recognize and make mathematically rigorous arguments.
Approach mathematical problems with curiosity and creativity and persist in the face of difficulties.
Work creatively and self-sufficiently with mathematics.
Understand and apply motivating examples that illustrate the ideas they are studying.
Apply mathematical ideas to problems in those areas of study.
See mathematical theory as useful and enlightening in both pure and applied contexts.
Recognize and integrate connections between mathematical courses and theory.
Use technology effectively, both as a tool for solving problems and exploring mathematical ideas.
Use technology with increasing sophistication throughout a major curriculum.
Be able to explore mathematical ideas and problems beyond the classroom.
Explore increasingly more difficult and open-ended questions.
Speak and write about mathematics with increasing depth and sophistication.

requirement_sections.heading: Language Requirement
content: The B.A. requires foreign language proficiency at the intermediate level.

requirement_sections.heading: Advanced Mathematics Courses
content: These courses are designed to give a breadth of knowledge in the three areas: Pure Mathematics, Applied Mathematics, and Statistical Modeling. These requirements also ensure a student has sufficient exposure to high level courses.
items: Courses in more than one group can be counted only once
With the approval of the Mathematics Department, MATH 4903 or MATH 4950 may be used as an Advanced course
Pre-practicum and practicum courses for teaching licensure are not advanced mathematics courses

requirement_sections.heading: Capstone Requirement
content: Choose ONE of the following (for a minimum of 3 credits):
* MAJs for IHIP, PL, and QR General Education Requirements
items: * MAJs for IHIP, PL, and QR General Education Requirements

requirement_sections.heading: Applied Mathematics Concentration
content: Applied Mathematics is the study of mathematics that is often used in other technical disciplines such as Chemistry, Biology, Engineering, Physics, Economics and Computer Science. Students who complete the concentration in Applied Mathematics will have a strong mathematical background, an emphasis in a second field and will be able to integrate the mathematics within that field.
The following must be completed with a minimum of 3 courses at the 4000-level.
items: Must take one additional Theoretical Mathematics course (3 credits)
Must take three Applied courses (9 credts)
Must take one Statistics course (3 credits)
Must take one additional Advanced course (3 credits)

requirement_sections.heading: Minor Requirement
content: Choose one of the following minors: Biology, Chemistry, Computer Science, Data Analytics, Economics, Geographic Science and Technology/Earth Systems Science, Psychological Science, or minor approved by the Mathematics Department.
